content.body:
Alkanes: CnH(2n+2) saturated
Methane CH4, Ethane C2H6, Propane C3H8, Butane C4H10
Reactions: combustion, free radical substitution with halogens (UV)

Alkenes: CnH(2n) unsaturated, C=C double bond
Ethene C2H4, Propene C3H6
Test for C=C: bromine water decolourises
Reactions: addition (more reactive than alkanes)

content.title: Key Reactions
content.body:
Alkane + Cl2 (UV): CH4+Cl2 → CH3Cl+HCl (substitution)

Alkene + H2 (Ni, 150 C): hydrogenation CH2=CH2 + H2 → CH3CH3
Alkene + Br2: addition CH2=CH2 + Br2 → CH2BrCH2Br
Alkene + H2O (H3PO4, 300 C, 65 atm): CH2=CH2 + H2O → CH3CH2OH

Cracking: long alkanes → shorter alkanes + alkenes

content.title: Structural Isomers
content.body:
Same molecular formula, different structures
C4H10: butane (straight chain) and 2-methylpropane (branched)
Branched isomers have lower boiling points

Naming: find longest chain, number from end nearest branch

questions.question: General formula of alkanes
questions.explanation: Alkanes saturated: CnH(2n+2). Each carbon forms 4 bonds with H or other C

questions.question: Bromine water decolourises with alkenes because
options: addition across C=C double bond (Br2 consumed)
questions.answer: addition across C=C double bond (Br2 consumed)
questions.explanation: CH2=CH2 + Br2 → CH2BrCH2Br. Orange Br2 colour disappears. Alkanes do NOT decolourise (no C=C)

questions.question: Alkane halogenation reaction type
questions.explanation: Free radical substitution: UV splits Cl2 homolytically, chain reaction replaces H atoms with Cl

questions.question: Hydration of ethene produces
questions.explanation: CH2=CH2 + H2O → C2H5OH. Industrial production of ethanol, H3PO4 catalyst, 300C, high pressure

questions.question: Hydrogenation converts alkene to alkane using
questions.explanation: Alkene + H2 → alkane over Ni catalyst. Used to make margarine (partially hydrogenated vegetable oil)

questions.question: Two structural isomers of C4H10 are
questions.explanation: C4H10 isomers: n-butane (straight) and 2-methylpropane (branched). Different boiling points: -1C vs -12C

questions.question: Cracking crude oil produces
options: shorter alkanes AND alkenes useful as fuels and monomers
questions.answer: shorter alkanes AND alkenes useful as fuels and monomers
questions.explanation: Cracking breaks long chains to shorter, more useful fractions. Alkenes produced are key monomers for polymer industry

questions.question: First four alkanes in order
questions.explanation: C1=methane, C2=ethane, C3=propane, C4=butane. Prefix: meth(1), eth(2), prop(3), but(4), pent(5), hex(6)

questions.question: Markovnikov addition: HBr + propene CH3-CH=CH2 gives
questions.explanation: Markovnikov: H adds to C with more H. In CH3-CH=CH2, H adds to =CH2 (more H), Br to =CH- giving 2-bromopropane

questions.question: Petroleum is separated into fractions by
questions.explanation: Crude oil fractions: different chain lengths, different boiling points. Fractional distillation separates by boiling point

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Alcohols: R-OH (hydroxyl group)
Primary alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid (K2Cr2O7/H2SO4, orange to green)
Secondary → ketone
Tertiary → no oxidation

Carboxylic acids: R-COOH (weak acids)
Esterification: acid + alcohol ⇌ ester + water (H2SO4 catalyst, reflux)
R-COOH + R-OH ⇌ R-COOR + H2O

content.title: Ester Synthesis
content.body:
Ethanol + ethanoic acid ⇌ ethyl ethanoate + water
CH3COOH + C2H5OH ⇌ CH3COOC2H5 + H2O
Conc H2SO4 catalyst, heat under reflux
Reversible: equilibrium mixture

Naming: alcohol part first (ethyl from ethanol), acid part second (ethanoate from ethanoic acid)
Smells fruity - used in flavourings, solvents

content.title: Tests
content.body:
Carboxylic acid + Na2CO3 → CO2 effervescence
Alcohol + K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 → orange to green (primary/secondary oxidise)
Fermentation: glucose + yeast → ethanol + CO2 (30C, anaerobic)

questions.question: Hydroxyl functional group is
questions.explanation: Alcohols contain -OH (hydroxyl) group. Methanol CH3OH, ethanol C2H5OH, propan-1-ol CH3CH2CH2OH

questions.question: Primary alcohol oxidised by K2Cr2O7 gives first
questions.explanation: Primary: -CH2OH → -CHO (aldehyde, distil off) → -COOH (acid, reflux). Dichromate: orange → green

questions.question: Tertiary alcohol cannot be oxidised because
options: no H on carbon bearing OH group needed for oxidation
questions.answer: no H on carbon bearing OH group needed for oxidation
questions.explanation: Tertiary: R3COH. No C-H bond adjacent to OH → no oxidation product possible

questions.question: Esterification is reversible. To improve yield, remove
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier: removing product shifts equilibrium right. Water absorbed by H2SO4 catalyst or removed by distillation

questions.question: Ethyl ethanoate is made from
questions.explanation: Ethyl = from ethanol. Ethanoate = from ethanoic acid (CH3COOH). Product: CH3COOC2H5

questions.question: Carboxylic acid + Na2CO3 gives CO2 gas. This proves
options: acid present (reacts with carbonate to form CO2)
questions.answer: acid present (reacts with carbonate to form CO2)
questions.explanation: 2RCOOH + Na2CO3 → 2RCOONa + H2O + CO2. Effervescence distinguishes carboxylic acid from alcohol or ketone

questions.question: Saponification makes soap by
options: hydrolysis of ester (fat/oil) with NaOH → sodium salt of fatty acid (soap) + glycerol
questions.answer: hydrolysis of ester (fat/oil) with NaOH → sodium salt of fatty acid (soap) + glycerol
questions.explanation: Fat (triglyceride) + 3NaOH → 3 fatty acid sodium salts (soap) + glycerol. Ancient process

questions.question: Biofuel ethanol is renewable because
options: crops absorb CO2 when growing then release same CO2 when burned - net zero carbon
questions.answer: crops absorb CO2 when growing then release same CO2 when burned - net zero carbon
questions.explanation: Carbon cycle: plants fix CO2 during growth. Fermentation and combustion release it back. Net carbon = approximately zero

questions.question: Fermentation conditions for glucose to ethanol
questions.explanation: Fermentation: C6H12O6 → 2C2H5OH + 2CO2. Anaerobic. Yeast active 25-40C. Above 45C enzymes denature

questions.question: Ethanol has higher boiling point than ethane despite similar molecular mass because
options: hydrogen bonding between O-H groups requires more energy to overcome
questions.answer: hydrogen bonding between O-H groups requires more energy to overcome
questions.explanation: OH group: strong intermolecular H-bonding. Ethanol bp=78C. Ethane (no OH) bp=-89C. H-bonds much stronger than London forces

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Addition polymers: C=C monomers
n(CH2=CH2) → (-CH2-CH2-)n poly(ethene)

Condensation polymers: two functional groups, lose H2O
Polyamides: -COOH + -NH2 → amide bond + H2O
Polyesters: -COOH + -OH → ester bond + H2O

Nylon-6,6: hexanedioic acid + hexane-1,6-diamine
PET: benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid + ethan-1,2-diol

content.title: Common Polymers
content.body:
Addition: poly(ethene) bags/bottles, poly(propene) rope, PVC pipes, PTFE non-stick, polystyrene packaging

Condensation: Nylon clothing/ropes, PET bottles/clothing, proteins (amino acids), DNA (nucleotides), starch/cellulose (glucose)

Problems: most synthetic polymers non-biodegradable
Solutions: recycling, biodegradable plastics, reduce use

content.title: Protein Structure
content.body:
Primary: amino acid sequence (peptide bonds)
Secondary: alpha helix or beta sheet (H-bonding)
Tertiary: 3D folding (ionic, H-bonds, disulphide, hydrophobic)
Quaternary: multiple polypeptide chains

Enzyme active site complementary to substrate - shape specific

questions.question: Addition polymerisation monomer must have
questions.explanation: Addition: C=C opens up, monomers chain together. No atoms lost. All monomer atoms in polymer

questions.question: Condensation polymerisation releases
questions.explanation: Each bond formed between monomers expels H2O molecule. Polymer chains grow as water is eliminated

questions.question: Nylon is a polyamide. Repeat unit linkage is
questions.explanation: Polyamides: -CO-NH- repeating units. Formed between -COOH and -NH2 groups with loss of water

questions.question: PET used in plastic bottles is a
questions.explanation: PET: poly(ethylene terephthalate). Condensation polyester from terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol. Recyclable code 1

questions.question: Synthetic plastics environmental problem: most are non-biodegradable because
options: microbes lack enzymes to break C-C backbone - persist hundreds of years
questions.answer: microbes lack enzymes to break C-C backbone - persist hundreds of years
questions.explanation: Evolutionary gap: synthetic C-C polymer backbones not present in nature before 1900s. Microbes have no enzymes evolved to break them down

questions.question: Degree of polymerisation: polymer Mr=56000, monomer Mr=56. n=
questions.explanation: n = Mr(polymer)/Mr(monomer) = 56000/56 = 1000 repeat units

questions.question: Cellulose and starch differ despite same glucose monomer because
options: starch has alpha-1,4 bonds (coiled, digestible), cellulose has beta-1,4 bonds (straight fibres, indigestible)
questions.answer: starch has alpha-1,4 bonds (coiled, digestible), cellulose has beta-1,4 bonds (straight fibres, indigestible)
questions.explanation: Alpha-glucose bonds allow coiling (starch). Beta-glucose bonds form straight chains that hydrogen-bond together (cellulose fibres, cotton, paper)

questions.question: PVC monomer is
questions.explanation: PVC = poly(vinyl chloride) = poly(chloroethene). Monomer: CH2=CHCl (chloroethene/vinyl chloride)

questions.question: Primary structure of protein is
options: sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds
questions.answer: sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds
questions.explanation: Primary structure: linear sequence of amino acids. Determined by DNA. All other structural levels derive from this

questions.question: Enzyme activity is reduced by high temperature because
options: enzyme protein denatures - active site loses complementary shape to substrate
questions.answer: enzyme protein denatures - active site loses complementary shape to substrate
questions.explanation: High T disrupts H-bonds, ionic bonds, disulphide bonds holding tertiary structure. Active site distorted - substrate no longer fits

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Organic Chemistry. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Organic Chemistry concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Organic Chemistry builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Organic Chemistry. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Organic Chemistry. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Organic Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Organic Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

Common examination questions on Organic Chemistry include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Organic Chemistry. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
